title: Maps and Geography: Continents, Oceans, and Directions
subject: social studies
blurb: Learn to read maps like an explorer — master continents, oceans, cardinal directions, and the tools that help us understand where everything is on Earth

vocab.definition: A flat drawing that represents an area from above — a tool for understanding location and distance
A round, three-dimensional model of Earth — more accurate than a flat map for showing true shapes and distances
One of the seven major landmasses on Earth: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, South America
One of Earth's five major saltwater bodies: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, Arctic
The four primary directions used for navigation: North, South, East, West
A symbol printed on maps showing the four cardinal directions — helps the reader orient themselves
Also called a legend — a box explaining what each color, symbol, and line on the map represents
A ratio on a map showing how map distance compares to real distance — e.g., 1 inch = 100 miles
An imaginary line circling Earth's middle, dividing it into the Northern and Southern hemispheres
Half of Earth — the equator creates Northern/Southern halves; the prime meridian creates Eastern/Western halves

teachingNotes: Use a physical or projected world map. Have students practice pointing to each continent and ocean. Reinforce cardinal directions by having them find things relative to each other on the map. The common mistake is confusing the equator (North/South divider) with the prime meridian (East/West divider).

sample.question: What is the difference between a map and a globe? When would each one be more useful?
Can you name all seven continents? Try listing them without looking — what do you remember?
Why is it important to check the map key before reading a map? What could go wrong if you skip it?
If you're facing North and turn completely around, what direction are you now facing? What about a quarter turn to your right from North?
The equator runs around the middle of Earth. Why is it hotter near the equator than near the North or South Pole?
Before GPS, how did sailors and explorers navigate across oceans? What tools did they use?
If a map's scale says 1 inch = 50 miles, and two cities are 4 inches apart on the map, how far apart are they in real life?

truefalse.question: There are seven continents on Earth.
The equator divides Earth into the Eastern and Western hemispheres.
A compass rose on a map shows direction.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
Australia is both a continent and a country.
South America is located north of North America.
A map key explains what the symbols and colors on a map represent.

multiplechoice.question: How many continents are on Earth?
Which is the largest ocean on Earth?
A compass rose on a map shows:
The equator runs directly through which continent?
What does a map key do?
Which continent is also entirely its own country?
If you travel west from the United States across an ocean, which ocean do you cross?

fillintheblank.question: The seven large landmasses on Earth are called ___.
North, South, East, and West are called ___ directions.
A ___ is a round, three-dimensional model of Earth.
The ___ divides Earth into the Northern and Southern hemispheres.
A map ___ is the box that explains what all the symbols and colors on a map mean.
The largest ocean on Earth is the ___ Ocean.

shortanswer.question: Name all seven continents. Which is the largest, and which is the smallest?
Explain the difference between cardinal directions and GPS. Why would someone still need cardinal directions today?
What is the equator, and why is the climate near it so different from near the poles?
Why does a map need a scale? What mistake could someone make if they used a map without one?
Name all five oceans. Which ocean do you think is most strategically important and why?
Why do the Northern and Southern hemispheres have opposite seasons at the same time?
How does a compass rose help someone navigate using a paper map? Walk through how you would use one.

wordProblems.question: You're looking at a map. You face North, then turn 180 degrees. What direction are you now facing?
A ship leaves Europe sailing west across the ocean. Which ocean does it cross to reach North America?
If you start in South America and travel east, which ocean do you enter?
A map's scale shows 1 inch = 100 miles. Two cities are 3.5 inches apart on the map. How far are they in real life?
A traveler flies from Africa heading east and lands in Asia. In which direction did she travel — east or west?
You live in the Northern Hemisphere. Your pen pal lives in the Southern Hemisphere. When you're enjoying summer vacation in July, what season is your pen pal experiencing?
An explorer wants to travel from the North Pole to the South Pole. What single cardinal direction do they travel the entire time?
